title: 1932: The Buffalo River Flood Control Debates
author: Historical Fact
content: Early 20th-century engineers proposed several large dams on Ozark rivers. The eventual rejection of these dams in favor of preservation saved the free-flowing nature of the Buffalo River.
action: Action: Before you 'dam' a river (automate/force a process), ask if the 'wild' flow provides more value than the controlled output.

title: 1811: The New Madrid Earthquakes
author: Historical Fact
content: A series of massive seismic events caused the Mississippi River to flow backward and created Reelfoot Lake. It remains one of the largest intraplate seismic events in history.
action: Action: Identify the 'tectonic' risks in your project—the rare, high-impact events you haven't planned for. Even if they are unlikely, acknowledge them.

title: 1940: The Creation of the Ozark National Scenic Riverways
author: Historical Fact
content: The establishment of the ONSR was the first time a national park system protected a 'river' rather than a landmark, preserving the Current and Jacks Fork rivers in their native state.
action: Action: Protect the 'process' (the river) more than the 'product' (the landmark). If the process is healthy, the products will follow.

title: 1840: The Natchitoches Trace
author: Historical Fact
content: A major pre-existing trade route used by Native Americans, then French traders, and finally American settlers. It defined the early geography of human movement through the Ozark hills.
action: Action: Use the 'paths of least resistance' that your predecessors have already carved out (existing libraries, templates, standard APIs).

title: 1950: The Rise of the Poultry Industry
author: Historical Fact
content: Corporate leaders like Tyson Foods and Walmart shifted the regional economy from self-subsistence farming to high-intensity industrial processing, permanently altering the labor market.
action: Action: If your project is about to go from 'hobby' to 'industrial', have you prepared for the change in scale and pressure?
